skill.name: executing-epic-workflow
skill.description: Guides structured feature development with the EPIC workflow: explore, research, plan, validate, implement, review, and iterate.
skill.summary: Coordinate complex feature work through a structured EPIC development workflow.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mainly false positives from Markdown backticks, file names, and ordinary workflow language. The real risks are limited to running a local Python validation script and creating local session report files; no prompt injection, data exfiltration, network call, credential access, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Local Validation Script Execution
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run a repository-local Python validator after each EPIC phase. The reviewed script validates phase names and required local files, and it does not spawn shells, call networks, or read secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly invokes a local Python script, so script execution is real. Confidence is high that impact is low because the script only validates arguments and checks local report files.

low_findings.title: Agent-Managed Local Session Files
low_findings.description: The workflow requires creating a .claude/sessions directory and writing phase reports there. This is expected for an audit trail, but it does create and validate local files inside the user workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and validation behavior is explicitly documented and implemented. The paths are local workflow artifacts, with no evidence of sensitive file targeting or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Matches Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings in SKILL.md and scripts/README.md are caused by Markdown code fences, inline paths, tool names, and example commands. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not interpolate untrusted input into a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are Markdown documentation, not executable Ruby or shell code. The only actual command is a documented Python validator invocation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match substrings such as Markdown file extensions, phase names, or descriptive text. The reviewed files do not implement hashing, encryption, certificate handling, or key management.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain workflow metadata, required report filenames, and a phase map. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm use is present in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Environment, Sensitive Key, and Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The env_access, certificate/key, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance alerts map to dictionary access, phase key listing, and research wording. No environment variables, certificates, private keys, host scanning, or network endpoints were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code uses PHASE_REQUIREMENTS.keys() and phase_config.get(), which are ordinary Python dictionary operations. The workflow mentions research but contains no concrete network or credential access behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Local Python Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow repeatedly instructs the agent to run a Python validation command from the skill directory. Users should inspect community scripts before running them, even though this script appears limited to local validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated in the workflow. Review of the script reduces severity because it performs local checks only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace File Creation and Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs agents to create session directories and required Markdown reports. This is normal for the workflow, but it is still filesystem modification inside the workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is directly stated in the instructions and implemented by path existence checks. No evidence suggests malicious file paths or destructive file operations.

content.value_statement: Complex feature work can lose context across planning, implementation, and review. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow with required reports and validation gates.
actual_capabilities: Guides feature work through explore, research, plan, validate, implement, review, and iterate phases.
Defines required reports for each workflow phase under a local session directory.
Delegates exploration, research, planning, validation, and review to specialized agents when available.
Keeps implementation work with the main agent after planning and validation are complete.
Provides a Python validator that checks whether required phase reports exist.
Requires iterative retries when a phase report is missing.
limitations: Requires compatible subagents or equivalent manual review steps to complete every phase.
Does not implement project-specific code by itself; it organizes the development process.
Creates local workflow documentation files that may need cleanup after completion.
The validation script checks file presence, not the quality or truthfulness of report content.

use_cases.title: Plan a complex product feature
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to gather context, research options, validate a plan, and implement only after review.

use_cases.title: Coordinate multi-agent code work
use_cases.description: Route exploration, research, planning, and review to specialized agents while keeping implementation controlled.

use_cases.title: Create an auditable delivery trail
use_cases.description: Generate required phase reports so decisions, findings, reviews, and final verification stay linked to the task.

prompt_templates.title: Start a small feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the EPIC workflow to add a small feature. Create the session directory, explore the codebase, plan the work, implement it, review it, and verify completion.

prompt_templates.title: Implement with validation gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use EPIC for this feature request. Do not implement until exploration, research, planning, and plan validation are complete. Run the phase validator after each phase.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate specialist agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use EPIC and delegate exploration, research, planning, validation, and review to the appropriate specialist agents. Save each report in the session directory.

prompt_templates.title: Run a strict delivery audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute the full EPIC workflow for a high-risk change. Require documented trade-offs, validation feedback, implementation notes, review findings, iteration, and final verification before completion.

output_examples.input: Add a new account settings page using the EPIC workflow.
output_examples.output: A session folder is created, context is gathered, a plan is validated, implementation tasks are tracked, review feedback is addressed, and final verification is documented.

output_examples.input: Use EPIC to refactor a payment integration safely.
output_examples.output: The workflow records current behavior, researches integration risks, plans the refactor, validates the plan, implements scoped changes, reviews quality, and confirms no regressions.

output_examples.input: Coordinate agents for a complex search feature.
output_examples.output: Specialist agents produce exploration, research, planning, validation, and review reports while the main agent performs implementation and final verification.

best_practices: Inspect community scripts before running the phase validator in a sensitive repository.
Use clear session names so reports are easy to find and review later.
Keep implementation scope aligned with the validated plan and document any required deviation.
anti_patterns: Skipping exploration or research and using the workflow only as a final checklist.
Treating file presence validation as proof that report content is accurate.
Letting specialist agents implement code outside the controlled implementation phase.

faq.question: What does EPIC mean in this skill?
faq.answer: EPIC means Explore, Research, Plan, Validate, Implement, Review, and Iterate. The skill applies these phases to feature development.

faq.question: Does this skill write code automatically?
faq.answer: It guides the process. The main agent still implements the code after planning and validation are complete.

faq.question: Why does it create session files?
faq.answer: Session files preserve context, decisions, plans, reviews, and final verification for the feature work.

faq.question: Can I use it without specialist agents?
faq.answer: Yes, but the user or main agent must perform equivalent exploration, research, planning, validation, and review steps.

faq.question: What does the validation script check?
faq.answer: It checks whether the required report files exist for each phase. It does not judge report quality.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: The reviewed script performs local file checks only. Still inspect community scripts before running them in private repositories.
